museum.name: Gaming Time Machine
museum.dek: The ZX Spectrum launches, Dig Dug spreads internationally, Pac-Man gets a promotional day, and the UK home-computer future becomes much cheaper and much noisier.
museum.curatorNote: April 1982 is one of the safest UK-aware months: the Spectrum launch is well documented, and Dig Dug's international release has strong date anchors.

items.title: Sinclair launches the ZX Spectrum
items.body: The Spectrum's low price, colour graphics and cassette ecosystem helped define the UK games industry that followed.
items.sourceNote: Centre for Computing History lists the ZX Spectrum launch on 23 April 1982.

items.title: Dig Dug reaches Europe
items.body: Namco's underground arcade game is listed for Europe on 19 April, with North American release also placed in April by several references.
items.sourceNote: Dig Dug references list Europe as April 19, 1982 and North America in April.

items.title: Atari promotes National Pac-Man Day
items.body: Atari's home Pac-Man campaign turned a cartridge into a cultural event, even as players debated the result.
items.sourceNote: AtariProtos and Pac-Man references identify April 3, 1982 as National Pac-Man Day.

items.title: UK micro software gets a new platform
items.body: The Spectrum created a market for small cassette games, bedroom coders, and mail-order software houses.
items.sourceNote: Derived from Spectrum launch and UK software industry context.

items.title: Arcade and home computing split into two futures
items.body: Arcades still had the glamour, but UK homes now had a cheaper programmable machine that could grow a games culture of its own.
items.sourceNote: Arcade/Dig Dug and Spectrum launch context.

sections.title: Releases
sections.subtitle: April gives Britain one of its defining hardware releases and arcades another hit.

items.title: ZX Spectrum
items.body: A hardware release rather than a game, but too important to leave off the release shelf.

items.title: Dig Dug
items.body: European arcade release of Namco's underground classic.

items.title: Pac-Man
items.body: The home cartridge sat at the centre of the month after March availability and April promotion.
items.sourceNote: March release and April 3 Pac-Man Day.

items.title: Ms. Pac-Man
items.body: Already one of the defining arcade presences of 1982.

items.title: Robotron: 2084
items.body: Moving from show debut toward operator memory as one of the year's most demanding cabinets.

items.title: Zaxxon
items.body: Its isometric view remained a showcase for arcade visual novelty.

items.title: Spectrum launch software
items.body: Early Spectrum programs were modest, but the platform made games feel writable and distributable.
items.sourceNote: Launch context; individual title dates not asserted.

items.title: BBC Micro listings
items.body: The BBC Micro remained the serious counterweight to the Spectrum's cheaper home-game promise.

sections.title: Hardware
sections.subtitle: Four objects define April: Spectrum, tape, arcade cabinet and Atari cartridge.

items.title: ZX Spectrum
items.body: A small black machine that made UK game creation feel local and affordable.

items.title: Cassette ecosystem
items.body: The Spectrum made the tape not just storage but retail packaging, distribution and ritual.

items.title: Atari 2600 cartridge economy
items.body: Pac-Man showed how cartridge licensing could become mass-market business and mass-market disappointment.

items.title: Arcade cabinet power
items.body: Dig Dug and Robotron reminded players that the arcade was still the premium games hardware tier.

sections.title: Magazine Covers
sections.subtitle: April's magazines had to explain a new British machine.

items.title: Spectrum launch coverage
items.headline: Sinclair's colour micro arrives
items.coverImageAlt: Reconstructed UK weekly computing cover for April 1982.
items.curatorNote: The Spectrum needed adverts, specs and listings before it had a mature games shelf.
items.body: The Spectrum needed adverts, specs and listings before it had a mature games shelf.

items.title: C&VG arcade/micro balance
items.headline: Dig Dug and the home-computer future
items.curatorNote: C&VG's hybrid identity fit April perfectly: arcades on one page, home machines on the next.
items.body: C&VG's hybrid identity fit April perfectly: arcades on one page, home machines on the next.

items.title: Personal Computer World
items.headline: Home micro choice expands
items.curatorNote: Serious computing coverage created the language games would borrow: memory, storage, graphics, price.
items.body: Serious computing coverage created the language games would borrow: memory, storage, graphics, price.

items.title: Electronic Games
items.headline: The arcade boom from across the Atlantic
items.coverImageAlt: Reconstructed Electronic Games cover for April 1982.
items.curatorNote: American games coverage gave UK readers another way to imagine the arcade market.
items.body: American games coverage gave UK readers another way to imagine the arcade market.

sections.title: Online Life
sections.subtitle: The Spectrum's network was a cassette and a magazine.

items.title: Listings became invitations
items.body: A printed BASIC program could be a game if you typed with care.

items.title: Mail order became discovery
items.body: Small adverts were portals to unknown software houses.

items.title: Arcade strategy stayed public
items.body: You learned Dig Dug by watching the good player before your turn.

items.title: There was no default online layer
items.body: For most UK players, information still arrived on paper.

sections.title: What It Felt Like
sections.subtitle: April sounded like a tape loading.

items.title: Games became domestic craft
items.body: The Spectrum suggested that a game could come from a bedroom, not just a factory.

items.title: Cheap mattered
items.body: Price changed who could imagine owning a computer.

items.title: The arcade still glowed brighter
items.body: Dig Dug had colour and presence a home tape could only chase.

items.title: The future had rubber keys
items.body: For Britain, the 1980s games industry suddenly looked small enough to fit under a TV.

sources.label: Dig Dug release context
sources.note: Used for April 19 Europe and April North America context.

sources.label: AtariProtos Pac-Man 2600
sources.note: Used for April 3 National Pac-Man Day context.

editorNotes: Initial AI-generated research draft; needs human editorial review and source verification.
